sentences.text: Not directly.
In other words, I didn't see Jews being taunted.
I heard slogans, and the singing, and the-- as I said, the marching and the general stuff that was in the newspaper.
And occasionally, you would hear somebody-- expression of dirty Jew, and, you know, stuff like that, but never directed at an individual.
I heard people say it.
Sometimes, in the schoolyard, I would overhear kids saying it.
Yeah.
But I never felt it was directed at me.
I don't know.
Maybe it was a certain blindness, but it was never directly-- you know, I wasn't around anyone who was individually assaulted.
No.
I never did.
Well, yes.
All the Jewish businesses were boycotted at the same time.
But when my father's business was actually closed and he was arrested, he was the only one.
Now, I did not see-- somehow, I guess I wasn't at home.
I must have been in school when my father was actually arrested, so I didn't see the soldiers coming to the house or the SA people or whoever it was, so I don't remember that.
I just don't remember my father being arrested.
I know he wasn't there.
And I know he was arrested.
And I know my mother being terribly upset about it, you know, and wondering what can be done.
And she didn't know where he was and where did they keep him.
And you know, it was-- we were all frightened.
We didn't know whether he was ever coming home or not.
But three days later, he came home.
I know he was gone for only three days.
And then he said that he was kept at the local jail, in Cologne, a place called the [NON-ENGLISH], whatever that may mean.
A name comes back.
Well, there was a radio.
We listened to the radio a lot.
Speaking of the radio, it reminds me of music.
We were-- even though no one in my immediate family at that time played an instrument, we all loved music.
My parents used to have a subscription to the opera.
I remember it, because, as a kid, I used to read all those librettos that they were always bringing home.
Before they went to see an opera, they always read the libretto, so that they would know the story.
And then, all of a sudden, they could no longer go to the opera.
Jews were not allowed to go to the opera.
And that was a blow.
And that was a way of curtailing things that they would normally do.
So we listened to the radio a lot.
We listened to music.
And, of course, there was the news on the radio, and you learned about all the new laws, and regulations, and restrictions, and so forth.
So the radio was always very important.
And of course, there were newspapers.
My parents read the newspaper.
I think there was-- I don't know why the name suddenly comes to mind.
Woo.
That's a mouthful, isn't it?
But you just reminded me.
I don't think I've thought of that word for a long time.
It means the West German-- Staats-Zeitung means official newspaper, you know.
Yeah.
No.
I think all the newspapers were anti-Semitic.
They were all government-controlled.
And I am here-- I didn't read the paper, so I'm not talking about my actually having seen it.
But it only stands to reason that they would be writing the official government position.
It was not like the though.
The was really the-- a horrible newspaper that was very racially-- what shall I say?
All the content was racial and they were out to destroy Jews in the most-- in the ugliest way.
You don't remember any Jewish newspapers being in the home?
No, I don't.
I don't.
There may have been, but I don't remember.
Yes.
It's the demonstrations on the Sportplatz I remember.
And you couldn't escape it.
We lived on the main street.
They would march down the main street.
And I remember, at one point, it was-- we were right near the main intersection of the main street, in the center of the village.
And they had a big banner across the square with anti-Jewish slogans on it.
So it was there.
It was there.
But it-- and I personally kept on moving in this protected cloud, which was transparent, but it didn't-- personally, no one threw me out of school.
I was able to do my homework and do my assignments, you know, like everybody else.
And, somehow, I was like in a little-- a cloud is probably the best way to describe it, because I could see out, but it didn't touch me personally.
Yes.
Yes.
I remember something about the Americans.
And there was a black winner and Hitler wouldn't shake the hands.
I forget the track star, I forget his name now.
And yeah, I remember the Olympics in Germany.
I would say it became progressively more hateful, general attitude.
But, at the same time, we were now starting to think about getting out.
And in a way, having a goal of getting out was very helpful, because we weren't just sitting there letting things happen.
The family knew that, you know, we were leaving.
I think my mother probably suffered the most, because she really became ill during this period.
And she went from doctor to doctor because she constantly had, quote, unquote, 'stomach trouble.' She couldn't eat certain foods.
She couldn't digest them.
She suffered a lot from diarrhea and was losing weight.
And she went from one big doctor to another.
And I remember them talking about operations.
My mother didn't want to be operated on in Germany.
I think some were Jewish and some weren't Jewish.
I'm not sure.
Yes.
I think that was-- I don't know when the Jews were no longer allowed to practice medicine.
I'm not sure that these were Jewish doctors.
I really don't know.
I only know she went to doctors.
And she went from one specialist to the other specialist, because, somehow, she did not want to go into a hospital and have surgery at this time.
And in the meantime, she kept on losing weight.
And she really was getting pretty sick with all of this going on, you know.
So that was a cloud that was over our heads.
And in the meantime, let's see.
Now, we finally got things going to come to the United States, I mean, in Germany.
Aunt Erna wrote that she was getting it all set up.
And then we had to wait in order for our quota to come up, so we could go to the American consulate in Stuttgart and get our American visa.
And all of that went well.
And, as luck would have it, I was the one who caused a problem.

When we had our health examination-- and this is at the American consulate-- they discovered that I couldn't see as well as I should be seeing, and that I couldn't come to the United States unless I had glasses.
Here we were in Stuttgart, which was probably the biggest trip I had ever taken at that point, because it was in southern Germany, and that was the nearest place for an American consulate.
And I needed glasses.
Well, no kid ever got glasses as fast as I did.
This was in the morning.
My parents took me out into the city somewhere.
We saw an optometrist.
And I don't know, but I got glasses within an hour or two.
And we went back to the consulate.
And they gave us-- gave me my visa.
And that was the big excitement in Stuttgart.
And we all had a visa to come to the United States now.
And it was time to make arrangements to really leave.
And we did that relatively quickly, considering that we got the visa in December-- on my passport, it's stamped December the 6th-- we very quickly put our house on the market.
I must say, the store was open again, so we had a business to sell.
And somebody local bought our house with the business.
Not a Jewish, a Christian family.
And not only that, they also bought a piece of property that we had, like a meadow, down by the river, just a piece of land.
And we sold that.
And they paid us, but then we couldn't take the money out of Germany.
So what we did, my parents bought a lot of stuff.
They really spent money like they never did in their life.
They bought an Oriental carpet, and, you know, things like that.
I don't remember exactly.
What stands in my mind is the carpet.
And we arranged for a lift to ship all our goods, all our belongings, furniture, linens, everything to the United States.
So this was packed in this huge crate.
And we moved out of our house approximately either just before or just after Christmas.
I know I went to school until the Christmas vacation started.
That was just before Christmas.
And my last day in school was actually very, very sweet.
I had this very nice teacher, Herr Mohr.
I mentioned him earlier.
And there was a Christmas party.
And each child had a candle on their desks.
And I was always amazed that, in retrospect, it certainly wouldn't be allowed in the United States in any classroom.
There were live wax candles that were lit on each child's desk.
And it was kind of dark.
And there was a Christmas tree in the classroom.
And everybody sang Christmas carols.
And I still remember singing 'stille nacht, heilige nacht' and it was so beautiful.
And when the party was over, and I think we all got candy, and everybody had left, I was the last child.
The teacher had told me to wait, because my father had notified the school that I was leaving.
And he gave me my report card, my book with all my grades in it.
And he kind of spoke to me very kindly.
He said he knew I was going to the United States.
And he wished me a lot of luck.
And he said, your father's a very wise man.
And that was the end of my school.
And I must say there were some-- there were some good people in Germany.
I think that teacher certainly was one of them.
I thought it was a great idea.
Yeah.
I never regretted leaving.
I thought it was a great idea.
I thought the idea of going to New York was just wonderful in my childish imagination.
And I think it wasn't just my imagination.
I think the cheapest place to live in Germany, where there aren't any elevators in buildings, was the top floor.
So my mother said, well, we'll probably be living in one of those skyscrapers right under the roof.
Little did she know about the expensive penthouses.
But we found out very quickly when we got here.
But we were so ignorant of what to expect here.
What was your image?
My image was that it was-- I had seen pictures of New York, obviously, you know, the skyscrapers.
It was such an exciting city.
It was nothing like I knew in Germany.
I had no idea what we were in for.
And to me, it was like a huge adventure.
I was a reader, and I had a very vivid imagination, but it wasn't always grounded in fact.
So this was like a fairy tale, going to a whole new world.
I think that was a consideration.
I think it was.
I think that we got that feeling from my parents.
And there was a sense of-- yeah.
Later on, I spoke to my sister.
And she was four years older than I.
And she always had the feeling, much more, that she was much more persecuted than I was.
And I don't know just exactly how she was persecuted.
Maybe it was because she couldn't go to the school that she wanted to go to.
Maybe it was because she went to a Jewish school and she just heard more-- more talk and heard of this one and that one.
But I'm afraid-- you know, I'm very happy, not afraid, that it just didn't touch me, personally.
It was there and I was conscious of it, but it didn't really touch me.
My parents were good.
I knew they loved me.
There was a secure family structure.
My-- the people who worked for us were kind and loving.
My teachers that I had contact with protected me.
I felt protected in school.
And that was good.
The people in the church were OK.
They certainly never attacked us in any way.
Neighbors would say, you know, Christian neighbors, well, I've known your father since he was a child.
You know, my father was so much part of this community.
Incidentally, my father served as a soldier in World War I.
He was a German soldier.
And he had typhoid in World War I.
He got very sick.
But he recovered and he was OK.
And he just couldn't believe that this really was happening.
He was a very loyal German.
And he was a part of this world.
And yet, somehow, he knew he had to get out.
And that was what saved us.
No.
They didn't sit us down.
They didn't talk to us.
It just wasn't done.
But we heard.
We heard them talking to the relatives.
We heard-- I-- I was the kind of child anyway-- and I don't know about other children.
I was a big listener.
I would sit and listen.
And, sometimes, people would tell me to go away.
I remember one incident where I had a little friend that I played with.
And his parents were talking one day.
And I just stood there and stared and listened.
And they told me, you know, go, go play.
And that was the kind of kid I was.
And I would take it all in and then build my own world based on what I heard.
And that is sometimes good and sometimes not so good.
But I always had a vivid imagination.
And what I wasn't sure of, I'd construct.
Absolutely.
It was going to be, you know, like Alice's adventures going through the looking-glass.
We were just going to leave all this and another world was going to open up for us.
And then we went home.
And it was a very busy time.
I mean, selling the house, selling the business, going out buying, buying, buying, buying.
I think, every day, my mother went shopping to take things, because we knew we weren't going to have anything when we got here.
I remember getting clothes that were in graduated sizes bigger than I was.
Oh, how I hated those clothes after I got here, because, in those days, kids didn't wear the same thing all over the world.
And it identified me as a refugee for years.
I couldn't wait to outgrow them.
And, you know, I had shoes.
I had everything.
She bought all this stuff, because we couldn't take any money out.
But we were sending this lift, so she was going to have clothes for us, and she was going to have linens to last forever, you know, bed linens, table linens.
She just bought, bought, and bought.
And so we packed up.
And then we left the house.
Now, we were supposed to leave for New York in the middle or the end of January.
It must have been the end of January, sure, because the-- we booked passage on the United States Lines.
My father was so wise that he did that because he wanted to be out of Germany as soon as he saw himself, you know, able to do that.
So once we were on the boat, we were going to be in United States territory, even though the boat was going to leave from Hamburg.
The United States Lines steamers at the time went from Hamburg to Le Havre and then to Cobh, which I believe is in England, and then on to New York.
They made these stops.
And we were scheduled to leave at the end of January to arrive in New York on February 17.
We had booked passage on that liner.
And in the meantime, my father was going to visit the relatives and, you know, and do things.
But we had to be at the house at the beginning-- before January 1st, because the new owners were going to take over.
So I went to stay with the relatives in Rheinbach.
Ketchen and Fritz were in Rheinbach.
And I loved those relatives, because that was also where my cousin Fritz, who was our apprentice, lived-- that's his parents-- and his older brother, Otto.
And I got along so well with those boys.
They were my favorite cousins.
I loved being with them.
I would trail after them.
They were about five, six, seven years older than I was.
I was a little girl.
They thought I was a little toy to play with, so they liked me and I liked them.
So I was looking forward to spending a few weeks with these relatives.
In the meantime, my mother and father and my sister stayed near Cologne in Bruhl, where my grandmother lived, with my mother's mother.
And she had a big apartment there and she had plenty of room for them.
My grandmother?
Henrietta Baer.
Henrietta was her first name.
And Baer, B-A-E-R, that's my mother's maiden name.
And she lived in the same building.
It was a rather large, nice apartment house with a dry goods store downstairs, with my aunt Frieda and my Uncle Max.
And I have a cousin, Bernie, Bernard, Bernd we called him  in Germany.
Bernd is still alive, living in New York.
And he and his parents came over here with my grandmother, actually, at the very end of 1939.
I think they were one of the last normal immigrants from Germany.
They still had a quota number that worked.
And they experienced the Kristallnacht in Germany.
And they had some other cousins who made out papers for them, on the other side of the family, the Salm side of the family.
Anyway, they came over here.
And that was very nice.
So my mother's family really was all saved, the immediate family.
Anyway, they were staying with the grandmother.
And I was in Rheinbach.
And then, one day, I was told to quickly pack up my things.
And my father was going to pick me up in [NON-ENGLISH], which is closer to Cologne and I had other relatives there.
Another aunt and uncle lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's right across the river from Bonn, so, you know, that was very close.
But it was halfway between Cologne and Rheinbach.
So my relatives from Rheinbach brought me over there immediately.
And my father was there.
And he said, we're leaving.
And I said, we're leaving.
I mean, what's happened?
I didn't know what was happening.
So he says, don't ask too many questions.
We're going right now to Cologne, where we'll meet your mother and sister.
And then we're getting on the train and we're going to Brussels.
We were leaving.
And this was certainly not in our plan.
And I didn't know what was happening at first.
But then, again, I heard the grown-ups talk.
My father explained it to his sister, whose house we were staying at in [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said that the day before-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he and my mother had been shopping in Cologne.
And they-- normally, when they go shopping in Cologne, they had coffee in the afternoon at a cafe where all the Jews used to congregate.
It was a Jewish meeting place.
And this was habitual.
They always went there for coffee in the afternoon if they were in the city.
And it seems that some stormtroopers had come to the house in Bruhl, where they were actually staying, at my grandmother's house.
And they knocked on Uncle Max's door, you know, his apartment.
And they said they were looking for Sally Wolf.
They heard that he was staying there.
